definition: Total money a company earns from selling goods or services before any expenses are deducted.
Profit remaining after all expenses, interest, and taxes are subtracted from revenue.
Another word for net income, often used interchangeably on earnings calls.
Net income divided by the weighted‑average number of shares outstanding; indicates profit per share.
EPS calculated assuming all dilutive securities (options, convertibles) are converted into common shares.
EPS based only on currently outstanding common shares, excluding potential dilution.
Informal term for revenue, referring to the first line on the income statement.
Informal term for net income, referring to the last line on the income statement.
Management’s forward‑looking estimates for future financial performance, such as revenue or EPS ranges.
Management’s qualitative and quantitative view of expected future performance and trends.
Comments about expected future results subject to risks and uncertainties, usually accompanied by legal disclaimers.
Average or median of analysts’ forecasts for metrics such as revenue or EPS.
When a company reports results above consensus expectations and also increases future guidance.
When a company beats expectations but keeps guidance unchanged.
When a company beats expectations but reduces future guidance.
Reported results that fall short of analysts’ or company‑provided expectations.
Three‑month reporting period used for financial reporting (Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4).
A company’s twelve‑month accounting year, which may not match the calendar year.
Change in a metric compared to the immediately preceding quarter.
Change in a metric compared to the same quarter or period in the prior year.
Extrapolation of current performance over a longer period, assuming current trends continue.
Growth generated from existing operations, excluding the impact of mergers, acquisitions, or currency movements.
Growth calculated using fixed foreign exchange rates to remove FX impact.
comps)
column3: Sales growth from locations open at least a year, excluding new stores or closures.

definition: Total value of signed contracts or orders during a period, regardless of when revenue is recognized.
Value of orders received but not yet delivered or recognized as revenue.
Set of potential deals or opportunities in progress but not yet booked.
Deliberate or natural reduction of a business portfolio as contracts mature and are not renewed.
Rate at which customers or recurring revenue are lost over a period.
Percentage of recurring revenue retained from existing customers after churn, upsell, and expansion.
Percentage of recurring revenue retained from existing customers excluding upsell and expansion.
Value of contracted recurring revenue normalized to a one‑year period.
Value of contracted recurring revenue normalized to a one‑month period.
Invoice amounts issued in a period, sometimes differing from recognized revenue.
Cash collected for goods or services not yet delivered; a liability until revenue is recognized.
Revenue earned but not yet invoiced to the customer.
Revenue minus cost of goods sold; profit after direct production or service costs.
Gross profit expressed as a percentage of revenue.
Profit from core operations after operating expenses but before interest and taxes.
Operating income as a percentage of revenue.
Earnings before interest and taxes; similar to operating income but may include some non‑operating items.
Earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization; proxy for operating cash generation.
EBITDA excluding certain items management deems non‑recurring or non‑core.
EBIT expressed as a percentage of revenue.
EBITDA expressed as a percentage of revenue.
Earnings before income tax expense is deducted.
Cash generated by operations minus capital expenditures; cash available to debt and equity holders.
Free cash flow as a percentage of revenue.
Net cash generated or used by the company’s core operating activities.
Cash spent on acquiring or upgrading long‑term assets such as property, plant, and equipment.
Current assets minus current liabilities; measure of short‑term liquidity.
Period‑to‑period movement in items like receivables, payables, and inventory that affects cash flow.
Degree to which a company uses debt financing, often expressed as a ratio to EBITDA or equity.
Total debt minus cash and cash equivalents.
Leverage ratio comparing net debt to EBITDA, indicating debt‑carrying capacity.
Ratio of operating income or EBITDA to interest expense, indicating ability to service debt.
Company’s ability to meet short‑term obligations, often discussed with reference to cash and credit facilities.
Financial or operational conditions attached to debt agreements that the company must comply with.
Mix of debt, equity, and other financing used to fund the business.
Net income divided by shareholders’ equity; measures profitability relative to equity base.
Net income divided by total assets; measures efficiency in using assets to generate profit.
After‑tax operating profit divided by invested capital; measures efficiency of total capital employed.
After‑tax operating profit minus a charge for the cost of capital; indicates value creation above capital cost.
Blended cost of debt and equity financing, often expressed as a percentage.
Average expected return required by all capital providers, weighted by their share of the capital structure.
Approximate time a company can operate before needing additional funding, based on current cash and burn rate.
Speed at which a company uses up its cash, typically expressed as cash spent per month or quarter.
Reduction in existing shareholders’ ownership percentage due to issuance of additional shares.
Company’s purchase of its own shares, reducing share count and potentially increasing EPS.
Cash or stock distribution paid to shareholders from profits or reserves.
Percentage of net income paid out as dividends.
Combination of share price appreciation and dividends over a period.
Increase in profit margins compared to prior periods.
Decrease in profit margins compared to prior periods.
Degree to which profit changes in response to revenue changes due to fixed versus variable cost structure.
Ability to increase revenue faster than costs, often leading to margin expansion as the business grows.
Cost savings or revenue benefits expected from a merger, acquisition, or integration.
Expenses associated with combining acquired businesses into existing operations.
One‑time costs related to reorganizing operations, such as layoffs or facility closures.
Write‑down of asset value when its recoverable amount falls below its carrying amount.
Non‑cash expense allocating the cost of tangible assets over their useful lives.
Non‑cash expense allocating the cost of intangible assets over their useful lives.
Non‑cash expense related to equity awards granted to employees and executives.
Performance measures that adjust GAAP results to exclude items management considers non‑core or non‑recurring.
Generally Accepted Accounting Principles; standard accounting rules under which official results are reported.
International Financial Reporting Standards; global accounting framework used outside some GAAP jurisdictions.
Distinct business line, geography, or product group reported separately within company results.
Proportion of revenue or profit generated from different regions or countries.
Composition of sales across products or categories, which can affect margins and growth.
Sales distribution across channels such as direct, online, and wholesale; can affect pricing and margins.
Average price at which a product or service is sold.
Number of units sold or amount of services delivered.
Change in proportion of products, customers, or geographies that affects revenue growth and margins.
Revenue and cost per individual unit, customer, or transaction used to assess profitability.
Average cost of acquiring a new customer, including marketing and sales expenses.
Estimated total profit a customer generates over their relationship with the company.
Time required to recover an investment, such as sales and marketing spend, from cash flows.
Number of employees, often discussed in the context of hiring freezes, reductions, or investments.
Reorganization of operations, often to cut costs or focus on core businesses.
Core set of products or services upon which additional offerings or ecosystems are built.
Company’s competitive advantage that protects profits from competitors.
External or internal factors that help growth or profitability.
External or internal factors that hinder growth or profitability.
Broader economic conditions such as GDP growth, inflation, and interest rates affecting the business.
Ability to raise prices without significantly reducing demand.
Management’s effectiveness in implementing strategy and delivering on commitments.
Clarity and predictability of future revenue or demand based on backlog, contracts, or indicators.
Perceived certainty around achieving future financial targets or milestones.
Additional qualitative detail or nuance requested by analysts beyond headline numbers.
Non‑recurring events such as asset sales or legal settlements that impact results for a single period.
Revenue that repeats regularly, such as subscriptions or maintenance fees.
Revenue generated from one‑off sales or non‑recurring transactions.
Contract under which customers pay periodically for ongoing access to products or services.
Right to use software or intellectual property, often linked with up‑front or periodic payments.
Post‑sale services such as updates, support, and warranties.
Total dollar value of all transactions processed on a platform before refunds or adjustments.
Percentage of transaction value that a platform retains as revenue.
Revenue retained after subtracting amounts passed through to partners or suppliers.
Average number of orders or transactions per customer over a period.
Number of users engaging with a product or service over a defined period.
Unique users who engage with the product on a typical day.
Unique users who engage with the product during a typical month.
Depth or frequency of user interaction, often measured by time spent or actions taken.
Group of customers sharing a common start date or characteristic used for behavioral analysis.
Percentage of customers or revenue that remains over time.
Loss of customers or revenue over time, the opposite of retention.
Number of times inventory is sold and replaced during a period.
Average number of days it takes to collect payment from customers.
Average number of days a company takes to pay its suppliers.
Network of suppliers, manufacturers, and logistics providers supporting production and delivery.
Maximum output a company can produce with its existing assets and resources.
Actual output as a percentage of available capacity.
Effectiveness in using resources to produce output, often tied to cost control and process improvements.
Efforts to reduce or better manage costs while preserving or improving performance.
Management’s focus on controlling spending relative to revenue and priorities.
Selling, general, and administrative expenses not directly tied to production.
Research and development spending on new products, services, or technologies.
Cost of goods sold; direct costs of producing products or delivering services.
Operating expenses, typically including SG&A, R&D, and other overhead.
Average tax rate on pre‑tax income based on actual taxes paid.
Portion of subsidiary earnings attributable to minority shareholders.
Alternative term for non‑controlling interests.
Per‑share figures such as EPS, cash flow per share, or book value per share.
Net asset value of the company (assets minus liabilities) attributable to shareholders.
Book value excluding intangible assets such as goodwill and trademarks.
Intangible asset arising from acquisitions when purchase price exceeds fair value of net identifiable assets.
Non‑physical assets such as patents, trademarks, and software.
Estimated market value of an asset or liability based on current conditions.
Recognition that an asset or receivable is no longer recoverable, removing it from the balance sheet.
Estimated liability recorded for expected future losses or obligations.
Reserve set aside for expected losses on receivables or loans.
Assessment of the likelihood that customers or borrowers will meet their obligations.
Collection of assets, customers, loans, or products managed together.
Assets adjusted for their risk level, used especially in banking to measure capital needs.
Measure of capital adequacy, such as equity or Tier 1 capital relative to risk‑weighted assets.
Net income divided by tangible shareholders’ equity, excluding goodwill and intangibles.
Management’s decisions on using cash for investment, debt repayment, buybacks, or dividends.
Key focus areas management emphasizes for long‑term value creation.
Management’s targeted long‑run ranges for growth, margins, and returns.
Segments where demand reacts quickly to economic changes and order cycles are brief.
Segments with long lead times between order and delivery, such as large projects.
Management’s view of future demand based on orders, backlog, and customer feedback.
Process of bringing inventory levels back in line with demand after an over‑ or under‑stock period.
Inventory held by distributors or retailers rather than by the company itself.
Informal or formal feedback from distributors, customers, or partners on demand conditions.
Discounting or marketing campaigns used to drive sales, often affecting margins.
Degree to which list price increases actually show up in realized selling prices.
Sensitivity of demand to changes in price.
Economic, political, or industry‑wide factors that negatively affect performance.
External trends that support or enhance performance.
Long‑term structural shifts that influence demand beyond normal cycles.
Performance patterns tied to economic cycles and short‑term fluctuations.
Early signs of improvement after a downturn.
Management’s description of weaker‑than‑expected demand or pricing.
Reducing debt levels relative to earnings or assets.
Increasing debt levels, often to fund acquisitions, buybacks, or investments.
Separation of a business unit into a new, independent publicly traded company.
Sale of a business unit, asset, or product line.
Relatively small acquisition that complements existing operations.
Large, strategic acquisition that significantly changes the company’s profile.
Transaction expected to increase EPS after completion.
Transaction expected to decrease EPS after completion.
Financials adjusted to show what results would have looked like assuming a transaction had occurred earlier.
Management’s claim that a change (restructuring, spin‑off, asset sale) will reveal or increase underlying value.
Rebalancing businesses or assets to focus on higher‑return or strategic areas.
Primary set of products or services that define the company’s main operations.
Businesses or assets considered outside the main strategic focus, often candidates for sale.
Effort to restore a struggling business to acceptable performance.
Moment when a meaningful change in trend or growth trajectory occurs.
Period in which production, sales, or hiring is increasing to reach a higher steady‑state level.
Phase where performance stops deteriorating and begins to flatten or improve.
Expanding operations, customer base, or production while seeking efficiency gains.
Strategy of starting with a small deal or footprint and growing with the customer over time.
Overall strategy for reaching and serving customers, including sales and marketing.
Specific approach or process by which sales teams engage and close customers.
Revenue or bookings generated per salesperson or per sales resource.
Frequency with which additional products or services are sold alongside primary offerings.
Selling additional products or services to existing customers.
Convincing customers to move to higher‑priced or higher‑value offerings.
Untapped opportunity within existing customer base or markets not yet fully penetrated.
Increases in a company’s market share relative to competitors.
Declines in a company’s market share.
Total potential revenue opportunity available in a market.
Portion of TAM that the company can realistically serve with its products and model.
Segment of SAM the company expects to capture in the near to medium term.
Extent to which the company has captured its target market, often expressed as a percentage.
Industry segment such as healthcare, retail, or financial services.
Product or service that can be used across many industries or customer types.
Combination of different operating or delivery models, such as cloud plus on‑premise.
Software or infrastructure deployed at customer locations rather than in the cloud.
Delivery of computing resources and software over the internet as services.
Software as a Service; subscription‑based software delivered via the cloud.
Increase in units sold, separate from price effects.
Contribution to revenue growth from changes in price and mix rather than volume.
Negative impact of foreign exchange movements on reported results.
Positive impact of foreign exchange movements on reported results.
Financial instrument or strategy used to reduce risk from currency, interest rate, or commodity price changes.
Company’s sensitivity to input prices such as oil, metals, or agricultural products.
Performance of hedging strategies in offsetting underlying exposures.
Earnings adjusted to remove unusual, cyclical, or one‑time items to reflect sustainable performance.
View of performance averaged over a full economic cycle rather than a single period.
Management emphasis on efficiency, quality, and continuous improvement in operations.
Strategic focus on customer needs, satisfaction, and long‑term relationships.
Company’s capacity to develop new products, services, or business models at scale.
Workforce quality and capability, especially in key technical or leadership roles.
Depth of talent available to step into critical positions if needed.
